q: Your team is building a production RAG pipeline on Azure. You need: a vector index over proprietary documents, a GPT-4o endpoint for completion, and an orchestration layer to chain retrieval with generation. Which Azure AI Foundry services map to each of these three concerns?
a: Azure AI Foundry is the unified platform that surfaces individual capabilities as distinct services you compose. For vector indexing over proprietary documents you use Azure AI Search with its built-in vector store and hybrid retrieval (keyword + semantic + vector); the Foundry portal exposes index creation through the 'Indexes' panel in your project. For the completion endpoint you deploy a model from the Foundry Model Catalog — GPT-4o is available as an Azure OpenAI managed deployment, giving you a Foundry-scoped endpoint and API key. For orchestration you use Prompt Flow, Foundry's visual DAG tool, or the azure-ai-projects SDK to wire a retrieval step (query the AI Search index) into a generation step (call the deployed model) as a single runnable pipeline. Foundry projects act as the container that holds connections to all three services — the connection object stores endpoint URIs and credentials, so your code references connection names rather than hard-coded secrets. For multimodal or grounding scenarios you can additionally bring in Azure AI Content Understanding to pre-process documents (extract layout, tables, images) before indexing, tightening retrieval quality.
fj: Azure Cognitive Search is a separate product unrelated to Foundry; you must connect to it via its own SDK outside of any Foundry project.
fs: Azure AI Search is a first-class connected resource inside a Foundry project. When you create or attach an AI Search resource in the Foundry portal, a connection object is stored in the project, letting Prompt Flow and the azure-ai-projects SDK address the index by connection name without managing credentials separately.

code.label: Create index + run retrieval via azure-ai-projects
lines: # List AI Search connections in project
# Run a vector query through the project's index

q: A data platform team asks you to choose between Azure AI Foundry Agent Service, Prompt Flow, and a plain Azure Function calling Azure OpenAI directly, for orchestrating a multi-step agentic workflow with tool use. What are the decisive selection criteria?
a: Foundry Agent Service (GA as of 2026) is the right choice when you need durable, stateful agents that maintain thread history, support tool calls (code interpreter, Bing grounding, Azure AI Search, Azure Functions as custom tools), and require enterprise oversight — the service manages conversation state in Azure Storage automatically and plugs into the Foundry Control Plane for runtime governance. Prompt Flow is better for deterministic, DAG-style pipelines where you want every step visible in a visual graph, easy evaluation runs, and batch testing against a dataset; it is not designed for open-ended tool-call loops. A plain Azure Function calling Azure OpenAI directly gives maximum control and lowest latency but requires you to build state management, retry logic, tool dispatch, and observability from scratch — appropriate only for simple single-turn scenarios or when you have bespoke routing needs Foundry cannot accommodate. The decisive criteria are: statefulness need (Agent Service wins), auditability of steps (Prompt Flow wins), unrestricted custom orchestration (raw function wins). For multi-step agentic workflows in an enterprise context, Agent Service is the intended answer on the AI-103 exam.
fj: Prompt Flow is the recommended choice for all agentic workloads because it supports tool calls and maintains conversation history natively.
fs: Prompt Flow is a pipeline tool — it does not natively maintain open-ended conversation threads or manage tool-call loops the way Agent Service does. Prompt Flow is best for fixed DAGs with deterministic inputs, while Agent Service handles the dynamic, stateful, tool-augmented reasoning loop that defines true agentic workflows.

q: You are deploying a Foundry Agent to production that has access to a SQL database tool and an email-sending tool. Your security team requires that the agent can never send an email without human approval and must only query tables in the 'reporting' schema. How do you enforce these constraints using Foundry governance features?
a: Foundry Control Plane exposes Agent Control Specifications — runtime guardrails that intercept execution at configurable points: user input, tool calls, tool responses, and output. To block unapproved email sends you configure a Human-in-the-Loop intervention on the email tool's tool-call event; the agent pauses execution and emits a review request to a designated approver endpoint before proceeding. To restrict SQL access to the 'reporting' schema you apply a tool-access constraint that rewrites or rejects queries whose SQL contains table names outside the allowed list — this can be implemented as a custom evaluation function registered with the Control Plane's tool-call hook, or by scoping the database credential used by the tool to a SQL login with schema-level READ ONLY permissions (defense in depth). Additionally, RBAC on the Foundry project controls which identities can modify the agent definition or tool list, and Key Vault-backed connection secrets prevent the agent from accessing credentials directly. Oversight mode can be set to 'supervised' so every tool invocation is logged to an Azure Monitor workspace for audit.
fj: Tool-access restrictions are only possible by modifying the system prompt to tell the agent which tables it is allowed to query.
fs: System-prompt instructions are advisory — a capable model may still generate SQL for disallowed tables if it infers them. Foundry Control Plane enforces constraints at the infrastructure layer by intercepting the tool-call payload before execution, which cannot be circumvented by the model's own reasoning. Always combine prompt-level guidance with platform-level enforcement for production agents.

q: Walk through the key steps to integrate an Azure AI Foundry generative workflow — including retrieval, prompt construction, and model invocation — into a Python web application using the azure-ai-projects SDK.
a: First, create an AIProjectClient authenticated with DefaultAzureCredential, pointing at your Foundry project endpoint (the hub URL plus project name). Second, retrieve the AI Search connection and the model deployment name from the project's connection registry using client.connections.get(), avoiding hard-coded credentials. Third, query your vector index using the client's indexes API or directly via the azure-search-documents SDK with the connection's endpoint and key, passing the user query and receiving top-k chunks. Fourth, build the prompt by inserting retrieved chunks into a system or user message alongside the original question. Fifth, call the chat completions endpoint using the azure-openai client obtained from client.inference.get_azure_openai_client(), which scopes the call to the project's deployed model. Finally, stream or return the response to the caller. The azure-ai-projects 2.2.0 SDK also exposes a beta routines API for scheduling repeated runs, and an evaluation API to run automated quality checks against a golden dataset before shipping updates.
fj: You must use the openai Python package pointed at api.openai.com to call Azure OpenAI models because azure-ai-projects does not wrap the completion API.
fs: azure-ai-projects includes client.inference.get_azure_openai_client(), which returns an AzureOpenAI client already configured with the project's endpoint and managed-identity credential. This means you never hard-code an AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT or AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY — the SDK reads them from the Foundry project connection.

code.label: Foundry SDK end-to-end generative call
lines: # 1. Get scoped Azure OpenAI client (no key needed)

q: A developer wants to generate an image of 'a futuristic skyline at dusk, oil-painting style' using Azure and also wants to allow users to upload a reference photo to influence the output style. Which service and model should they use, and what API parameters control the reference image influence?
a: As of 2026, DALL-E 2 and DALL-E 3 have been retired by OpenAI; the current model on Azure OpenAI is gpt-image-1 (also called GPT Image), deployed through Azure AI Foundry. GPT Image supports both pure text-to-image generation and image editing with a reference image — the image edits endpoint accepts the original image as a PNG upload alongside the text prompt, and optionally a mask image to constrain where edits are applied. For style influence from a reference photo you call POST /openai/deployments/{model}/images/edits with form fields: image (the reference PNG), prompt (the style description), n (number of images), size, and quality. If you want unconstrained style transfer rather than in-painting, you pass the whole image without a mask. gpt-image-1 also has a generate endpoint (POST /images/generations) for pure text-to-image. Both endpoints return base64-encoded PNG data or a URL depending on the response_format parameter. You deploy the model through the Azure AI Foundry Model Catalog under the Azure OpenAI provider section.
fj: DALL-E 3 is still the current model on Azure and is the correct choice for all new image generation tasks.
fs: DALL-E 2 and DALL-E 3 were retired on May 12, 2026. Deployments of those model versions no longer accept new traffic. The replacement is gpt-image-1 (GPT Image 1.5 generation), which also unifies text-to-image and image-editing into the same deployment, whereas DALL-E 3 only supported text-to-image.

code.label: Image generation with reference style (gpt-image-1)
lines: prompt='A futuristic skyline at dusk, oil-painting style',

q: Your product team wants to add AI-generated video clips to a marketing platform on Azure. Describe how to submit a text-to-video job with Azure OpenAI Sora 2 and poll for completion.
a: Azure OpenAI Sora 2 is available in preview through Azure AI Foundry as a deployed model under the Azure OpenAI provider. Video generation is asynchronous — you POST a job request to the video generations endpoint with a JSON body containing prompt (the text description), width, height, n_seconds (duration, currently 5 or 10 seconds in preview), and n (number of clips). The API responds immediately with a job ID and status 'queued'. You then poll GET /openai/deployments/{model}/video/generations/{job_id} until status becomes 'succeeded', which typically takes 1–5 minutes. On success the response includes a download URL or base64 content depending on your output_type parameter. Error states include 'failed' (model refused the prompt, content policy violation) and 'cancelled'. Sora 2 uses the same recaptioning technique as DALL-E 3 — prompts are internally enriched before generation, so detailed descriptive prompts produce higher-quality output than terse ones. Reference video or image conditioning is not yet GA in Sora 2 preview.
fj: Azure Video Indexer is the correct service for generating videos from text prompts on Azure.
fs: Azure Video Indexer is an analysis service — it extracts metadata, transcripts, and insights FROM existing videos, not generate new ones FROM text. Text-to-video generation on Azure uses Azure OpenAI Sora 2, which is a generative model deployment accessible through the Foundry portal and the Azure OpenAI REST API.

code.label: Submit and poll a Sora 2 video job
lines: 'prompt': 'A drone flyover of a neon-lit city at night, cinematic',

q: Your accessibility team needs your app to generate both a brief one-line alt-text and a richer multi-region description for images uploaded by users. Which Azure AI Vision API features do you use, and what is the difference between Caption and Dense Captions?
a: Both features are part of Azure Vision in Foundry Tools (Image Analysis API v4.0 GA). Caption generates a single English sentence describing the entire image; it is returned in the captionResult field when you include 'Caption' in the visualFeatures request parameter. Dense Captions goes further — it generates up to ten individual captions, each with its own bounding box (boundingBox coordinates in pixels), allowing you to describe distinct regions of an image rather than a single holistic summary; this is the denseCaptionsResult field. For accessibility, the recommended pattern is: use Caption as the img alt attribute (concise, screen-reader friendly) and Dense Captions to power a 'detailed image description' tooltip or expandable section for users who want more context. Both features run through the same POST /computervision/imageanalysis:analyze endpoint with the features query parameter. Note: as of the 4.0 API, captions are only available in English and only from Vision resource regions that support the Florence model (East US, West US, etc. — not all regions).
fj: Dense Captions produces a paragraph summary of the whole image with more adjectives than the regular Caption feature.
fs: Dense Captions does not produce a single paragraph — it produces up to 10 separate bounding-box-scoped captions, each describing a spatial region of the image. This region-level granularity is what makes it useful for accessibility and for downstream grounding tasks where you need to cite which part of an image a description refers to.

code.label: Caption + Dense Captions via Image Analysis 4.0

q: You are building a support chat application where users can share a screenshot of an error and ask 'What does this error mean?' How do you implement visual question answering (VQA) grounded in a user-supplied image using Azure AI services?
a: The correct Azure service for visual question answering grounded in an actual image is Azure OpenAI's GPT-4o (or GPT-4 Turbo with Vision), which accepts multi-modal input: text and image(s) in the same chat message. You send a chat completion request where the user message content is an array — one element is a text object (the question) and one or more elements are image_url objects pointing at the image as a base64 data URI or a publicly accessible HTTPS URL. The model reasons over the image content and produces an answer that is grounded in what it sees. For a screenshot use case the base64 data URI approach is preferred so you do not need to host the image publicly. Azure Vision Image Analysis v4.0 also has a separate VQA-style capability under the ObjectDetection and Caption features, but it is purpose-built for object presence queries, not open-ended natural-language reasoning about arbitrary content. For production, add Azure AI Content Safety to screen both the uploaded image and the model's response for policy violations before returning output to the user.
fj: Azure Vision Image Analysis v4.0 is the recommended service for open-ended visual Q&A because it has a dedicated VQA endpoint.
fs: Azure Vision Image Analysis v4.0 supports caption and object detection queries but does not support open-ended natural-language reasoning like 'What does this error message mean?'. For open-ended grounded visual Q&A you need a multimodal LLM — GPT-4o on Azure OpenAI — which receives both the image and the question in the same prompt and can reason across arbitrary visual content.

code.label: GPT-4o visual Q&A with base64 screenshot
lines: {'type': 'text', 'text': 'What does this error mean and how do I fix it?'},

q: Your team is publishing images generated by an AI art tool and needs to auto-generate WCAG-conformant alt-text at upload time. What Azure service and API pattern should you use, and what implementation detail matters for WCAG conformance?
a: Azure Vision in Foundry Tools Image Analysis v4.0 has an explicit alt-text use case: call the analyze endpoint with the 'Caption' visual feature and set the gender_neutral_caption=True parameter to produce inclusive language aligned with WCAG Success Criterion 1.1.1 (Non-text Content). The returned text is a single English sentence that concisely describes the dominant visual content of the image. For WCAG conformance the implementation detail that matters most is that decorative images should receive an empty alt attribute (alt='') rather than a generated caption — you should apply a pre-filter (for example, flag images tagged as 'abstract pattern' or 'texture' by the Tags feature) to route them to empty-alt rather than caption generation. Additionally, WCAG guidance requires that alt-text not be redundant with adjacent visible text — if the image is illustrative of a paragraph already in the page, you should either suppress the alt-text or summarize only the visual-only information. The caption API only returns English text; for multilingual sites you should pass the output through Azure AI Translator to localize the alt-text before storing it.
fj: Dense Captions is the correct feature for alt-text generation because it produces richer descriptions.
fs: Alt-text is intentionally concise — a long multi-region description would overwhelm screen readers and violate the WCAG principle of being succinct. The Caption feature (single sentence) is the appropriate choice for an img alt attribute, while Dense Captions is more suited to extended-description blocks (aria-describedby) for users who want to explore image detail beyond the brief alt-text.

q: Explain how Azure AI Content Understanding in Foundry Tools handles visual understanding tasks that go beyond the Image Analysis v4.0 API, and describe when you would choose Content Understanding over Image Analysis for a production pipeline.
a: Azure AI Content Understanding (GA November 2025) is a multimodal service that unifies document intelligence, image understanding, video understanding, and audio understanding in a single API surface, orchestrated through Azure AI Foundry. Where Image Analysis v4.0 excels at specific tasks — captioning, object detection, OCR, tagging — Content Understanding uses generative AI reasoning to answer complex, open-ended questions about visual content, extract structured fields from images following a custom schema, and chain across modalities (for example, combine transcript text with video frame analysis). Content Understanding exposes prebuilt analyzers (prebuilt-imageSearch, prebuilt-videoSearch, prebuilt-audioSearch) and lets you define custom analyzers with a fieldSchema that specifies which fields to extract and their types. You choose Content Understanding over Image Analysis when: (1) you need schema-based field extraction from images (invoices, diagrams, forms) rather than generic tags or bounding boxes; (2) you need cross-modal reasoning (what a speaker said at the moment an on-screen graph appeared); (3) you want a single API that handles documents, images, and video in the same pipeline for downstream RAG. Image Analysis remains the lighter, lower-latency choice for simple captioning, OCR, or object detection on images.
fj: Content Understanding is simply a rebranded version of Image Analysis v4.0 and exposes identical capabilities.
fs: Content Understanding is architecturally distinct from Image Analysis. It uses LLM-based reasoning to fill user-defined schemas, supports video and audio modalities, and is managed through Foundry project connections — it is not a rename. Image Analysis v4.0 remains a separate resource type optimized for fast, task-specific computer vision (OCR, caption, objects) without requiring an LLM inference call.

code.label: Custom image analyzer with Content Understanding
lines: from azure.ai.contentsafety import ContentUnderstandingClient  # preview package
# Define a custom analyzer for product label images
'description': 'Extract product name and expiry date from label images',

q: You need to build a pipeline that ingests a library of 2-hour training videos, generates timestamped summaries of each scene, and makes the content searchable. Which Azure service handles video segmentation and analysis, and what prebuilt analyzer do you use?
a: Azure AI Content Understanding in Foundry Tools provides the prebuilt-videoSearch analyzer, which accepts a video file URL and returns segment-level results: each segment includes a start and end timestamp, a textual summary of the scene, and optionally a transcript if speech is detected. The service performs automatic scene detection to determine segment boundaries, so you do not need to provide timestamps manually. For a 2-hour video you submit an async analysis job and poll for completion — the job returns a JSON document with a contents array where each element represents one detected segment. To make the segments searchable you feed the resulting JSON into Azure AI Search: map the segment text to a searchable field and the start_time_ms/end_time_ms to metadata fields so that search results can deep-link to the correct video timestamp. For speaker identification within segments, you can combine Content Understanding with Azure AI Speech's speaker diarization to attribute dialogue to named speakers. Alternatively, Azure Video Indexer (a separate service) provides similar timeline-based analysis with its own web portal and API but does not integrate into the Foundry project connection model as directly.
fj: Azure Media Services is the correct Azure service for video segmentation and scene detection.
fs: Azure Media Services (the classic streaming/encoding platform) was retired. For AI-driven video understanding — scene segmentation, transcript extraction, and content search — the current Azure solution is Azure AI Content Understanding (prebuilt-videoSearch analyzer) within Foundry Tools, or Azure Video Indexer for scenarios that need a dedicated video analysis portal.

q: A retail platform processes store-camera images to detect out-of-stock shelves, identify brand logos, and flag shoppers in restricted zones. Which Azure AI Vision capability handles each detection scenario?
a: Azure Vision in Foundry Tools Image Analysis v4.0 covers three distinct detection surfaces. For object detection (out-of-stock shelf areas, brand logos as visual objects) you use the Objects visual feature, which returns a list of detected objects with bounding box coordinates and a confidence score — product packaging and logos are detected as object instances when they fall within the model's built-in taxonomy, and custom object models can be trained via Azure AI Vision's Custom Vision integration for domain-specific SKUs. For brand logo identification specifically, the Brands visual feature (a separate visualFeature value) returns recognized commercial brands with bounding boxes. For detecting people in restricted zones — tracking presence and movement — you use Azure AI Vision Spatial Analysis, which runs as a Docker container on Azure IoT Edge or AKS at the camera edge; you configure zones as polygon coordinates in the operation JSON, and the service emits events when a person enters or exits a zone, with optional speed and orientation attributes available in the 2025 API version. Content Safety image moderation is the right add-on if the scenario also requires filtering inappropriate images before they are stored or displayed.
fj: Azure Custom Vision is the only way to do object detection on Azure; the built-in Objects feature only detects generic categories like 'car' and 'person'.
fs: Image Analysis v4.0's built-in Objects feature detects hundreds of object types with reasonable accuracy and does not require training. Custom Vision is the right choice when you need to detect proprietary SKUs, specific branded packaging, or domain objects that are not in the built-in model taxonomy — the two features are complementary, not mutually exclusive.

q: Your platform allows user-generated image uploads and must block hate symbols, graphic violence, and CSAM before storing images. Describe how to implement this filter using Azure AI Content Safety and what the severity level model returns.
a: Azure AI Content Safety exposes an image analysis endpoint (POST /contentsafety/image:analyze) that classifies an uploaded image against four harm categories: Hate, SelfHarm, Sexual, and Violence. Each category returns a severity score from 0 to 6 using a four-level severity scale: 0 (Safe), 2 (Low), 4 (Medium), 6 (High). You configure reject thresholds per category — for example, blocking Sexual at severity >= 2 and Violence at severity >= 4 depending on your platform's policy. The API accepts the image as a base64 string or a blob URL. For CSAM (child sexual abuse material) the service applies unconditional refusal and logs the request regardless of threshold configuration. In code you inspect the categoriesAnalysis array in the response and raise a validation error before writing to storage if any category exceeds your threshold. For high-volume workloads the service supports batching via the Azure AI Content Safety client SDK. Additionally, you can enable the Prompt Shield and Groundedness checks for any text associated with the image upload (captions, tags) to catch indirect prompt injection via image metadata.
fj: Azure AI Content Safety only works on text; you need Azure Custom Vision to classify images as safe or unsafe.
fs: Azure AI Content Safety has a fully supported image moderation endpoint distinct from its text moderation endpoint. It uses the same Florence-based vision model stack and returns per-category severity scores for visual content. Custom Vision is a classification training tool — it does not come pre-trained with harm taxonomies for safety-critical use cases like CSAM detection.

code.label: Image harm classification with Content Safety

q: You are building a voice-enabled customer support agent on Azure that must transcribe customer speech in real time, feed the text to a GPT-4o reasoning step, and return a spoken response. Describe the end-to-end pipeline using Azure Speech in Foundry Tools and where custom speech models fit.
a: The recommended architecture at Build 2026 is the Voice Live API in Azure Speech (Foundry Tools), which creates an end-to-end voice agent pipeline combining STT, LLM reasoning, and TTS in a single low-latency WebSocket connection — this avoids the round-trip latency of separate API calls. If you require finer control, the manual pipeline is: (1) Azure Speech SDK continuous recognition with the SpeechRecognizer in streaming mode to transcribe each utterance, sending interim and final results via events; (2) the final transcript text is passed to Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o) for intent resolution and response generation; (3) the response text is synthesized using Azure Neural TTS via SpeechSynthesizer. Custom speech models are applicable at step 1 — if your callers use domain jargon (medical terminology, product SKU codes) that degrades standard STT accuracy, you train a custom acoustic or language model in Azure Speech Studio, deploy it as a custom endpoint, and specify that endpoint ID in the SpeechConfig. The LLM Speech API (GA 2026) is an alternative for asynchronous long-form transcription using a specialized LLM-powered recognition model with lower hallucination rates, but it is not real-time.
fj: Azure Cognitive Services Conversation Transcription is the recommended approach for all real-time voice agent scenarios.
fs: Conversation Transcription is optimized for multi-speaker meeting transcription with diarization — it identifies who said what in group conversations. For a customer support agent where the primary concern is low-latency single-stream STT feeding into an LLM, the Voice Live API (or the continuous SpeechRecognizer + OpenAI pipeline) is the correct architecture.

code.label: Continuous STT with Azure Speech SDK

q: Your team is integrating a custom domain-adapted speech model into a Foundry Agent so the agent can handle voice interactions. What is the process to train and deploy a custom speech model, and how do you wire it as an agent modality?
a: Custom speech model training in Azure Speech Studio follows these steps: (1) create an Azure Speech resource and open Speech Studio; (2) upload training data sets — acoustic training data (audio + transcript pairs in .wav/.txt format) for acoustic adaptation, or language model data (domain-specific text corpus) for vocabulary and pronunciation adaptation; (3) train the custom model, selecting the base locale model (e.g., en-US base); (4) test the custom model against a held-out audio set and measure Word Error Rate; (5) deploy the model as a custom endpoint in your Speech resource and note the endpoint GUID. To wire it as a Foundry Agent modality, you register the Azure Speech resource as a connection in your Foundry project and configure the agent's voice channel with the custom endpoint ID in the speech_config portion of the agent definition — in the azure-ai-projects SDK this is an AgentVoiceConfig object specifying the speech resource connection name and endpoint_id. The Voice Live API also accepts a custom_endpoint_id parameter so the end-to-end pipeline uses your fine-tuned recognition model instead of the base model, improving accuracy for product names, part numbers, or medical terms specific to your domain.
fj: Custom speech models must be called directly via the Speech SDK; they cannot be referenced through a Foundry project connection or used by a Foundry Agent.
fs: Foundry Agents support a voice modality that can reference custom speech endpoints via the agent's voice configuration. The Foundry project connection model abstracts the credential management, so the agent definition includes the connection name and endpoint ID rather than raw subscription keys.

q: A user uploads an audio recording of a meeting and asks a GPT-4o-based assistant to answer questions about it. How do you enable multimodal audio reasoning on Azure, and what are the current model and API constraints?
a: GPT-4o on Azure OpenAI supports audio input (as of the gpt-4o-audio-preview deployment available since late 2024, now in preview GA). You send a chat completion request where a user message content element is of type input_audio — the audio field contains the base64-encoded audio bytes and a format value ('wav' or 'mp3'). The model can then answer questions about the audio content in its reply. Constraints to know: the audio modality is available only on specific deployment types (gpt-4o-audio and gpt-4o-realtime-preview); maximum audio duration varies by model version (commonly up to ~10 minutes per turn); and the feature does not replace dedicated transcription — for long-form audio requiring a verbatim transcript, Azure Speech LLM API (GA 2026, up to 5-hour files) is more accurate and cost-effective. For a Q&A flow over a 1-hour meeting you would first transcribe with Azure Speech LLM API, then ask GPT-4o text questions about the transcript, rather than sending raw audio to GPT-4o.
fj: You must first transcribe audio with Azure Speech and then pass the text to GPT-4o; GPT-4o cannot directly process audio files on Azure.
fs: GPT-4o on Azure does support native audio input via the input_audio message content type on the gpt-4o-audio deployment. The nuance is that for long audio files (>~10 minutes) or where transcript accuracy is critical, pre-transcription with Azure Speech LLM API gives better results than raw audio input to the model. Both paths are valid; the right choice depends on audio duration and accuracy requirements.

code.label: GPT-4o audio input for multimodal reasoning
lines: {'type': 'text', 'text': 'What action items were agreed in this meeting?'},

q: Your company operates a multilingual contact center and needs to translate customer speech from Spanish, Japanese, and German into English in real time for English-speaking agents. Describe the Azure solution and the relevant SDK classes.
a: Azure Speech translation in Foundry Tools provides real-time speech-to-translated-text conversion in a single API call. You create a SpeechTranslationConfig, set speech_recognition_language to the source language (e.g., 'es-ES'), and add target languages using add_target_language() (e.g., 'en'). A TranslationRecognizer is instantiated with this config and an audio input source. As the recognizer processes speech, the Recognized event returns a TranslationRecognitionResult whose translations dictionary maps each target language code to the translated text in real time. For multilingual callers where the source language is unknown, you enable auto language detection (SourceLanguageConfig with multiple candidates) so the model selects the source language from the first few seconds of speech. The LLM Speech API (GA 2026) extends this with LLM-powered translation across 25 languages in batch mode for post-call analytics — more accurate for technical or idiomatic speech but not real-time. Custom translation models from Azure AI Translator can be integrated by specifying a custom endpoint in the translation config for domain-specific terminology.
fj: You need Azure AI Translator (text translation) and Azure Speech separately — there is no combined speech translation service.
fs: Azure Speech includes a built-in speech translation capability that performs STT and translation in a single pipeline without a separate Translator API call. SpeechTranslationConfig and TranslationRecognizer handle the full flow. Azure AI Translator is used when translating already-transcribed text or when you need document translation, not for streaming audio.

q: Your Azure AI Search pipeline ingests PDF contracts, but they contain scanned pages, handwritten notes, and embedded tables that built-in OCR misses domain entities like contract clauses and party names. Describe how to implement custom enrichment using Azure AI Search skillsets to address each gap.
a: Azure AI Search skillsets chain built-in and custom skills in a directed acyclic graph. For scanned pages, the built-in OcrSkill extracts text from images embedded in or generated from PDF pages using Azure Vision OCR; you set detectOrientation=true and point the skill at the normalized_images field produced by the ImageAnalysisSkill or the indexer's imageAction=generateNormalizedImages configuration. For handwritten notes, OcrSkill also handles handwriting when the source image quality is sufficient — set the textExtractionAlgorithm to 'handwritten' on regions known to contain handwriting. For embedded tables, the LayoutAnalysis capability (available via the built-in DocumentIntelligenceLayoutSkill, added in newer Search API versions) preserves table cell structure that plain OCR flattens. For domain entities (contract clauses, party names), a custom WebApiSkill calls an Azure Function that invokes an Azure OpenAI or Language custom NER model on the merged text and returns structured entity outputs. The skillset output fields are mapped to the index schema using outputFieldMappings. Finally, a MergeSkill concatenates all extracted text fragments so the full document text is searchable as a single field.
fj: Custom skills can only add structured metadata; they cannot feed text back into the index's searchable content fields.
fs: Custom skill outputs are mapped to index fields through outputFieldMappings just like built-in skill outputs. If a custom skill returns an extractedText string, you map it to a searchable text field in the index and it becomes full-text searchable. There is no restriction on the type of output a custom skill can contribute to the index.

code.label: Skillset snippet: OCR + custom NER skill

q: Describe how to build a multimodal extraction pipeline on Azure that ingests a batch of insurance claim PDFs containing text paragraphs, photographs of damage, and form tables, and produces a structured JSON record per claim for downstream processing.
a: The recommended architecture combines Azure AI Content Understanding and Azure AI Document Intelligence (both now in Foundry Tools). For each PDF: (1) Use the Document Intelligence Layout model (prebuilt/layout, GA) to produce a full structural analysis — it returns paragraphs, tables (with cell content and position), selection marks, and figures (bounding boxes of embedded images), all in markdown or JSON format. (2) For each figure extracted by the layout model, feed the image bytes to Azure AI Content Understanding using a custom image analyzer with a fieldSchema that targets claim-relevant fields (damage_type, severity, affected_area). This step uses LLM-based visual understanding rather than rule-based tagging. (3) For structured form fields (policy number, claimant name, date of loss) use Document Intelligence's prebuilt/idDocument or a custom model trained on your form layout to extract typed fields with confidence scores. (4) Merge the layout text, visual field extractions, and form fields in application code (or an Azure Function) into a unified JSON record keyed by claim ID. Store in Azure Blob Storage and optionally index in Azure AI Search for retrieval. This pipeline replaces a previously complex stack of separate OCR, Computer Vision, and Form Recognizer calls with two focused services.
fj: Azure Form Recognizer (Document Intelligence) alone can handle photographs of physical damage because it has a built-in visual damage detection model.
fs: Document Intelligence's prebuilt and custom models are designed for text-and-table extraction from structured documents — they do not include domain-specific visual damage classification models for insurance claims. Photographs of damage require a vision or content understanding model trained or prompted to identify damage categories. The correct approach is to use Document Intelligence for document structure + Content Understanding (or GPT-4o Vision) for photographic content.

code.label: Document Intelligence layout extraction
lines: print(result.content)          # full markdown with tables preserved
for fig in result.figures:     # iterate embedded images

q: You are building a RAG system where source documents are scanned PDFs, PowerPoints with embedded charts, and audio recordings of analyst calls. Explain how Azure AI Content Understanding's prebuilt analyzers produce clean grounded representations for each modality, ready for chunking and indexing.
a: Azure AI Content Understanding provides three prebuilt analyzers that normalize different modalities into a uniform, index-ready representation. For scanned PDFs and office documents, prebuilt-read and prebuilt-layout extract clean markdown text with table structure, headings, and reading-order preserved — critically, the output is 'grounded' in that each text span carries a reference back to its source page and bounding region, enabling precise citation in RAG answers. For PowerPoints with embedded charts, prebuilt-imageSearch applied to the slide images (or using the multimodal document understanding path) generates descriptive text about chart content and visual elements, turning otherwise unsearchable visuals into searchable text chunks. For audio recordings, prebuilt-audioSearch performs transcription with speaker diarization and produces a timestamped transcript — each segment includes a speaker label and start/end time so the RAG system can cite the exact moment in the recording that grounded an answer. You then chunk each analyzer output using Azure AI Search's integrated vectorization (split_mode=pages or sentences), embed the chunks with an Azure OpenAI embedding model, and store them in an AI Search vector index. The grounding metadata (page number, bounding box, timestamp) is stored as metadata fields and returned with search results so the application can render source citations.
fj: Content Understanding output must be post-processed by custom code to extract any grounding metadata; the raw analyzer output is plain text only.
fs: Content Understanding is specifically designed to emit grounded representations — the output JSON includes bounding regions for document text, timestamps for audio segments, and visual region references for image content. This grounding metadata is a first-class output, not something you derive in custom code, and it is why Content Understanding is recommended over plain OCR for RAG pipelines that need trustworthy source citations.

code.label: Audio grounding with prebuilt-audioSearch
lines: # Each segment has speaker + timestamp grounding
